input.userRequest: Recommend which Briefing Genie actions can be automated and which should require human approval.

messages.content: You are Briefing Genie. Return a short briefing with source citations.

User request: Recommend which Briefing Genie actions can be automated and which should require human approval.

Source packet: Human Approval Boundary Packet

Packet summary: Notes about when AI-generated operational recommendations can act automatically versus require review.

Sources:

[S1] Governance workshop
Type: workshop notes
Participants approved automatic drafting, summarization, and artifact linking when outputs are clearly marked as generated. They rejected automatic policy changes, customer commitments, and release blocking without a human approver.

Workshop participants drew a boundary between preparation and authority. Drafting, summarizing, and linking artifacts were acceptable because a human could inspect them before action. Policy changes, customer commitments, and release blocking were rejected because they change obligations or stop work without accountable approval.

[S2] Operations incident note
Type: incident review
A prior automation posted a recommendation as if it were an approved decision, causing two teams to pause rollout work unnecessarily. The incident review asks for explicit status labels: draft, recommendation, approved action, and blocked.

The incident note describes an automation that posted a recommendation in a channel where readers assumed it was an approved decision. Two teams paused rollout work before anyone confirmed the recommendation. The incident owner asked for status labels that distinguish draft analysis from approved action.

[S3] Product analytics
Type: analytics digest
Users are most likely to accept generated briefings when the interface separates evidence, recommendation, and action controls. Acceptance drops when the recommendation and execution button appear in the same visual cluster.

Analytics showed higher acceptance when evidence, recommendation, and action controls were visually separated. Sessions with a combined recommendation-and-action cluster produced more cancellations and clarification clicks. The product team interpreted that as a sign that users need time to inspect evidence before acting.

[S4] Legal review
Type: legal review
Any automated message that could be interpreted as a customer commitment needs an accountable human reviewer. Legal approved internal draft summaries as long as they cannot be confused with final commitments.

Legal approved generated internal drafts because they remain advisory. The same review rejected automated customer-facing commitments without an accountable reviewer. The legal note asks the product to make it impossible to confuse generated summaries with final commitments.

[S5] Action taxonomy draft
Type: taxonomy draft
The action taxonomy draft divides Genie behavior into generated drafts, recommendations, approved actions, and blocked actions. Drafting and artifact linking are listed as safe defaults. Release blocks, customer commitments, policy changes, and external messages require an approver recorded in the artifact trail.

[S6] Interface review notes
Type: interface review
The interface review notes warn against placing an execution button directly beside an AI recommendation. Reviewers want the evidence panel, recommendation panel, and action controls separated so users understand what is generated and what has been approved. The note recommends a visible status label before any action can be taken.

output.title: Automation vs. Human Approval Boundaries for Briefing Genie Actions
output.summary: Automate “preparation” work (drafting, summarization, evidence collection/linking) as default, but require explicit human approval for any action that creates obligations, changes policy, sends external/customer-facing messages, or blocks work (e.g., releases). Enforce clear status labeling and UI separation between evidence, recommendations, and execution controls to prevent recommendations being mistaken for approved decisions.

claims.text: Automate drafting, summarization, and artifact linking when outputs are clearly marked as generated, because these are preparatory and meant for human inspection before action.
Require human approval for policy changes, customer commitments, and release blocking, since these create/modify obligations or stop work and need accountable authorization.

A prior incident showed that posting a recommendation as if it were an approved decision can cause unnecessary work stoppage; use explicit status labels (draft, recommendation, approved action, blocked) to prevent misinterpretation.

User acceptance is higher when evidence, recommendation, and action controls are separated; combining recommendation and execution controls leads to more cancellations and clarification clicks, suggesting users need time to inspect evidence before acting.

Legal requires an accountable human reviewer for any automated message that could be interpreted as a customer commitment; internal draft summaries are acceptable only if they cannot be confused with final commitments.

openQuestions: Which specific action types in your current Genie roadmap map to 'external messages' (e.g., vendor emails, customer tickets, status-page posts), and should any be allowed as auto-drafts only?
What is the minimum required “approver recorded in artifact trail” mechanism (identity, timestamp, scope, rationale) for approved actions?
What default channel/visibility rules should apply so recommendations cannot be mistaken for approved decisions (especially in high-traffic team channels)?
Which UI/UX pattern will you standardize on for separating evidence, recommendation, and action controls, and what are the gating conditions before enabling execution?
output.recommendation: Automate by default: (1) generating draft briefings/summaries, (2) extracting and organizing evidence, (3) linking artifacts and assembling audit trails—always with prominent generated/draft labeling. Require human approval (with approver recorded) for: (a) policy changes, (b) release blocks or any action that pauses/stops work, (c) customer commitments and any customer-facing/external messages, and (d) any action that changes obligations. Implement a strict state model (draft → recommendation → approved action / blocked) with visible status labels, and keep evidence, recommendation, and execution controls visually and interaction-wise separated so execution cannot be conflated with an AI recommendation.

pricing.source: OpenAI API pricing, standard short-context rates, 2026-06-23
